studyMetadata.title: Exploring Deuteronomy
studyMetadata.subtitle: Bible Study
studyMetadata.author: Julie Rowbory
studyMetadata.shareMessage:
Check out this Bible study app — “Bible Study Reader”.

Available soon on Google Play and the App Store.

theme.theme-annotation: 1 John study theme — soft gold and ivory palette. Gentle, luminous, and reassuring, reflecting themes of light, truth, love, and confident assurance in faith.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: 1 John for Today
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
A six-session Bible study on the book of 1 John.

Work through each chapter at your own pace, or use it with a group.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Read, reflect, write
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Each chapter has Bible questions tied to specific passages, and personal reflection questions at the end. Tap ⓘ beside any Bible reference to read the passage without leaving the app.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Saved on your device
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Your answers are saved automatically as you type, and stored on this device. Use the Share button at the bottom of each chapter to send your answers to a group leader or mentor.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Adjust to your preference
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you need bigger text go straight to settings now
studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: Tap the ⚙ icon in the top corner to open Settings any time to change text size or switch to dark mode. Tap ☰ (contents) to jump to any chapter.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: How to use this app
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you want a full guide to getting the most from this app and this study – whether on your own or in a group – you can tap the button below.

action.label: Go to the ‘How to use’ page →

studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: You can also find it any time via the ? icon in the top navigation bar.

blocks.title: People who struggle with assurance
blocks.body: If you find yourself wondering whether you are really a Christian — whether your faith is genuine, whether God truly accepts you, whether you really belong to him — this letter was written for you. 1 John is not a test designed to produce anxiety. John states his purpose plainly at the end: ‘I have written these things to you who believe in the name of the Son of God so that you may know that you have eternal life’ (5:13). The goal is confidence, not doubt.

blocks.title: People unsettled by false teaching
blocks.body: John wrote into a specific crisis: people had left the community, and their departure had shaken those who remained. If you have ever been confused by confident-sounding teaching that seemed to undermine what you believed, or by Christians who seemed more certain and more impressive but whose message felt subtly different, this letter speaks directly to that experience.

blocks.title: People who want to go deeper in 1 John
blocks.body: The letter is only five chapters long, but it is theologically rich and structurally complex. This study moves through it carefully, paying attention to what John actually says in context rather than lifting out familiar verses in isolation. Whether you are new to 1 John or have read it many times, working through it closely is likely to surface things you have not noticed before.

blocks.title: On your own
blocks.body: This study works well as a personal study. Each session begins with a prompt to read the relevant passage — take that seriously, since the questions are designed to reward close attention to John’s actual words. Write your answers as you go, and give the reflection questions at the end real time. 1 John rewards slow, attentive reading more than speed.

blocks.title: In a group
blocks.body: The study works well in a small group setting. The most rewarding approach is usually for each person to work through the session individually first, writing their answers, and then for the group to come together to compare what they found. The themes of light, love, and assurance generate rich discussion — and because John’s letter was written to a community, reading it together has a particular fittingness.

blocks.title: Read the whole letter first
blocks.body: 1 John is short enough to read in one sitting — about twenty minutes. The first chapter of this study includes the full text, and you are encouraged to read or listen to all five chapters before beginning. Seeing the whole letter at once makes each individual session significantly richer, and helps you see how John’s recurring themes build and interweave.

blocks.title: Working with a leader or mentor
blocks.body:
If someone has recommended this study to you — a pastor, a small group leader, or a trusted friend — that relationship matters. 1 John raises personal questions about assurance, love, and what genuine faith looks like in practice. Having someone to think those questions through with can make the difference between the letter producing its intended comfort and it becoming a source of unnecessary anxiety.

At the end of each chapter, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your leader or mentor — via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone. This gives them a window into your thinking before you meet, and makes for a much richer conversation.

blocks.title: Two kinds of questions
blocks.body: Each session has two kinds of questions. The Bible questions follow the passage closely — asking you to look carefully at what John actually says, in context. The reflection questions at the end turn the same material toward you personally, asking what John’s message means for your own faith and life. Both kinds matter: the Bible questions build the foundation; the reflection questions are where the letter does its pastoral work.

blocks.title: Keeping John’s purpose in view
blocks.body: Some verses in 1 John can feel challenging or even alarming when read in isolation — especially those about sin, love, and what it means to ’remain’ in God. As you work through the questions, keep John’s stated purpose in mind: he is writing to give assurance to believers, not to undermine it. When a verse feels difficult, the first question to ask is: how does this fit with what John is trying to achieve?

blocks.title: The ’tests’ in 1 John
blocks.body: You may have encountered approaches to 1 John that present it as a series of ’tests’ — of belief, love, and obedience — to apply to yourself. This study takes a different approach. Those same markers are best understood as tools for evaluating the false teachers who had unsettled John’s readers, not as a personal checklist for assessing your own standing. That reorientation makes a significant difference to how the letter reads.

intro: These notes are written for leaders and mentors guiding others through an expository study of Deuteronomy. Unlike a topical study, an expository study asks you to stay close to the text — to let the passage set the agenda rather than jumping too quickly to applications or cross-references. Your main job is to help people read carefully and ask good questions of what they find. Deuteronomy rewards that kind of attention: it is a richer, more theologically dense book than it first appears, and these notes are designed to help you lead it well.

**Know what kind of book you are leading people through.** Deuteronomy is not primarily a law code — it is a collection of sermons. Moses stands on the plains of Moab, on the edge of the Promised Land he will never enter, and preaches to the generation that is about to cross the Jordan. That preaching context matters: the laws and commands come embedded in a relationship, a history, and a pastoral urgency. Moses is not a legislator delivering a statute book; he is a dying pastor pleading with the people he loves to stay faithful to the God who has been faithful to them. Help your group feel that weight.

**The structure of the book is your friend.** Deuteronomy has a carefully designed architecture — three sermons, a song, and a blessing, with editorial sections providing historical context. Within that, the book makes extensive use of chiastic (mirror-image) structure, where sections echo each other and the central element carries particular weight. You do not need to teach the structure as a lecture, but knowing it will help you see what the author is emphasising in any given passage and why certain themes keep returning.

**Work through the passage yourself before you lead.** Every session in this study is asking people to look closely at the text — at specific words, structures, and patterns. You will lead that observation far better if you have done it yourself first. Don't just read the questions; read the passage. Ask yourself what you notice. What surprises you? What is Moses most urgent about here? What would you want someone to explain?

**The Background section in each session's notes is for you, not necessarily for the group.** It gives you the exegetical and contextual grounding to handle tricky questions with confidence. You don't need to deliver it as a lecture — but knowing it will help you stay calm when a question lands that you weren't expecting. The same applies to the exegetical notes: they are a resource for you, not a script.

**Watch for the person who is overwhelmed by the distance between the text and their life.** Deuteronomy contains passages that feel very remote — laws about warfare, food regulations, procedures for handling murder cases, curses pronounced in elaborate ritual. Some people will struggle to see what any of it has to do with them. Your job is not to collapse that distance artificially, but to help the group move patiently from 'what did this mean for Israel then?' to 'what does this reveal about God?' to 'how does that shape us now?' That three-step movement takes time, but it is the honest and fruitful way through.

**Don't rush the text to Jesus — but don't leave him out.** Deuteronomy is quoted more often in the New Testament than almost any other Old Testament book. Jesus himself quotes it three times in the wilderness temptation. Moses' role as prophet, mediator, and intercessor points unmistakably forward to Christ. The connections are real and important — but if you jump to them too early, you rob people of the experience of reading the Old Testament on its own terms. Let the passage do its own work first, and then follow where it leads.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 1–3: Disobedience Only Leads to Failure

background.body:
**Setting and context.** Deuteronomy opens with a precise geographical and temporal note: the people are camped east of the Jordan, in the fortieth year, in the eleventh month. They are days away from entering the land. That context gives Moses' opening sermon its urgency — this is not a theoretical review of history; it is a last-chance briefing before the crossing.

**Moses' first sermon (1:6–4:40).** The opening chapters of that sermon (chs 1–3) are essentially a selective retelling of the wilderness journey, focusing on two episodes of rebellion and the faithfulness of God in between. Moses is not giving a comprehensive history; he is choosing episodes that will make a specific theological and pastoral point to people who are about to face their own moment of decision.

**The two rebellions.** The first rebellion (1:19–46) is the refusal to enter the land at Kadesh Barnea, rooted in fear and doubt about God's goodness — despite everything they had seen him do. The punishment is severe: that entire generation will die in the wilderness. But God does not abandon his people; their children will inherit the promise. The second rebellion (1:41–46) is almost the mirror image of the first: presumptuous disobedience after being told not to advance. Fear and presumption are presented as two faces of the same failure — both represent a refusal to trust God on his own terms.

**God's faithfulness in the wilderness (ch 2).** Sandwiched between the rebellions and the account of military victories is a reminder that even in the wilderness, God was providing, protecting, and directing — not just for Israel but even for the surrounding nations. This is a significant theological point: the Lord is sovereign over all nations and all history, not just Israel's.

**Moses and the land.** The end of chapter 3 is deliberately sombre. Moses will not enter the Promised Land — 'because of the people' (3:26), a recurring phrase in Deuteronomy that presents Moses' exclusion in a different light to the Numbers account (Num 20). This ambiguity is worth sitting with: Moses is a great leader, but he bears consequences that are not entirely his own — an anticipation of the pattern of the suffering servant and ultimately of Christ.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: disobedience only leads to failure, so trust in the faithful, covenant God and obey his word.** Moses is not simply recounting history for its own sake. He is making a case: look at what happened when your parents didn't trust God. Look at what God did anyway. Now you are standing where they stood. Choose differently.

**Fear and presumption are both failures of trust.** The first rebellion is driven by fear of the inhabitants of the land — the people look at the giants and conclude that God cannot or will not deliver them. The second is driven by presumption — they think they can correct their mistake by going up anyway, without God's blessing. Both stem from the same root: treating God as less than he is. Help your group see that these are not opposites but twins.

**God's faithfulness despite human failure.** Chapter 2 is a remarkable corrective to the gloom of chapter 1. The Lord has been with them through forty years of wilderness, blessing them, providing for them, directing even their interactions with neighbouring nations. His faithfulness is not contingent on their faithfulness. This is meant to encourage the next generation — and the group you are leading — to trust him.

**The solidarity of the people of God (3:12–20).** The Reubenites and Gadites who have already received their land east of the Jordan are not permitted to settle there until the rest of the people have taken theirs. The people of God are one people. They do not pursue individual blessing at the expense of the community. This is a quietly significant point that rewards reflection.

**Moses points forward to a greater mediator.** The sombre ending of chapter 3 — Moses seeing the land he will not enter — raises a question the book will develop: if even the greatest leader of God's people falls short, who will ultimately bring the people home? The New Testament's answer is worth holding in reserve, but the question is planted here.

pastorals.body:
**People will identify with the fear of the Israelites.** The first rebellion is very human. The people had genuine reasons for anxiety — the inhabitants really were powerful, the task really was daunting. Don't let the group dismiss the Israelites as simply faithless. Help them see that the failure is not in feeling afraid, but in allowing fear to override what they knew to be true about God. That is a distinction that will land personally.

**The second rebellion is harder to handle pastorally.** Presumption — the idea that we can undo disobedience by acting as if it hadn't happened, or that God will help us regardless of what he has said — is a subtle and common temptation. Some in your group may have done something similar: decided after the fact that God would bless what they chose, without reference to his word. Handle this gently but honestly.

**The consequences of others' sin.** Moses bears consequences partly rooted in the people's rebellion. Some in your group will have experienced something similar — carrying the weight of others' failures, paying a price that doesn't feel entirely their own. This passage doesn't resolve that tension, but it names it. Acknowledge it if it comes up.

**Don't let the military content dominate.** Questions about the conquest of Canaan and the dispossession of peoples will arise, and they are worth addressing briefly (see exegetical notes). But this session's focus is on the pattern of trust and disobedience, not on the ethics of holy war. Keep the main thing the main thing.

exegetical.body:
**'Because of you' / 'because of the people' (1:37; 3:26).** Moses says the Lord was angry with him 'because of you' — that is, because of the people's rebellion. This sits in some tension with Numbers 20, where Moses' own sin (striking the rock) is given as the reason. Deuteronomy does not contradict Numbers; it presents the matter from a different angle, highlighting Moses' solidarity with the people he led. Both dimensions are probably true: Moses sinned, and he did so in a context of being ground down by decades of the people's grumbling. Worth acknowledging the complexity rather than flattening it.

**The spies (1:19–25).** The people's request to send spies is presented by Moses as originating with the people ('you came to me', v22), whereas Numbers 13:1–2 presents it as the Lord's instruction. Again, both can be true: the people proposed it, God permitted it. The key point in Deuteronomy is that even the spies' positive report ('it is a good land', v25) could not overcome the people's fear — which makes the rebellion all the more culpable.

**The defeat at Hormah (1:41–46).** The irony of the second rebellion is sharp: the people first refuse to go up when God tells them to, then insist on going up when God tells them not to. The Lord is not with them in either act of disobedience. This is not random punishment — it is the logical consequence of choosing to act outside of God's word.

**God's sovereignty over surrounding nations (2:1–23).** The land given to Moab, Edom, and Ammon is described as the Lord's gift to them — not as the result of their own power or virtue. This is a remarkable claim: Yahweh, Israel's God, is the sovereign distributor of nations and territories across the whole earth. The lesson Moses draws from this is direct: if God can do this for these pagan nations, how much more will he do it for his own people?

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group getting stuck on the ethics of the conquest — the destruction of peoples, the military violence. These are legitimate questions and you should acknowledge them honestly, but they are not the focus of this session. A brief response ('Moses' concern here is not to justify the conquest but to show what happens when Israel trusts God and what happens when they don't') will usually be enough to redirect. If someone wants a deeper conversation about the conquest, offer to pick it up separately.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The key themes of this session — fear vs trust, God's faithfulness in the wilderness, the solidarity of God's people, and Moses as a flawed mediator pointing forward — are covered in the Background, Key Points, Pastoral Sensitivities, and Exegetical sections above. The notes below focus on guiding the group through the text question by question.)*

---

### 1:1–5 — The Setting for the Book

**Q1. What is the geographical and historical setting for Deuteronomy? Why is this significant? What does Moses do in this context?**

The people are east of the Jordan, at the very end of the fortieth year — on the cusp of entering the land. Moses expounds the law to them, reminding them of what they need to know to obey God and experience his blessing. The precision of the setting matters: this is not a general theological lecture. It is urgent, pastoral, and timely.

**Q2. What does God want the people to do, and why?**

To go and possess the land — because he promised it to their ancestors. The promise to the patriarchs is the ground of the command. They are not being asked to take the land by their own strength or virtue; they are being asked to receive what God has already committed to give.

---

### 1:6–18 — Leadership of the People of God

*(This section is discussed in small groups using the handout — see below. Brief notes are provided here for the leader.)*

**What do vv6–8 show us about the character of God?**

God is the one who initiates — 'You have stayed long enough at this mountain.' He directs the movement of his people, sets the boundaries, and gives the land. He is not passive or distant; he is actively ordering the life of his people.

**What does vv9–18 say about what God values? How will these values be picked up later in the book?**

The establishment of a justice system — fair judges who show no partiality, hear small and great alike, and do not fear anyone — reflects God's own character. These values recur throughout Deuteronomy, especially in chs 16–17 and 19. The people of God are to be characterised by justice because their God is just.

**Why was it important for the Israelites to be reminded of this leadership structure at this stage in their history?**

They are about to enter a new phase of national life in the land, with new challenges and new temptations. Good structures of leadership and justice are not incidental — they are the backbone of a community that reflects God's character. Moses is handing these structures on deliberately before he leaves.

---

### 1:19–46 — Disobedience and Failure

**Q4. In vv19–21, what are the people told to do? What reasons for obedience are given?**

To take the land and not be afraid or discouraged. The reason is repeated: God is *giving* them the land (note how this idea recurs in vv20, 21, 25). He is fulfilling his promises — so they should obey and have no reason to fear. The logic is simple: if God is giving it, what is there to fear?

**Q5. Why did the people want to send spies? What was the result?**

To find out the best route. Ultimately, although the spies confirmed the land was good, their report about the inhabitants discouraged the people. What should have been confirmation became a source of fear — because they were looking at the obstacles rather than at God.

**Q6. What was the people's first rebellion? What was at its root? How did Moses try to dissuade them?**

They refused to enter the land. The root was fear of the inhabitants and doubt about God's goodness and ability. Moses urged them not to be afraid by rehearsing everything God had done in Egypt — he is a God who fights for his people. The argument is: look at the track record. Why would he stop now?

**Q7. What was their punishment? How did God still show his love? How did the people respond to the punishment?**

None of that generation was allowed to enter the Promised Land, except Caleb and Joshua, who had trusted God (see Numbers 14 for fuller background). But the children will enter — God does not cut off his people completely. The people's response is revealing: they change their minds and try to go up anyway, apparently thinking this will change God's decision. It will not.

**Q8. What was the people's second rebellion? What was at its root?**

They fought the Amorites despite being told not to, and that the Lord would not be with them. The root is presumption — the assumption that God will always help them regardless of what he has said. This is not faith; it is a different kind of faithlessness.

**Q9. What was the consequence? How did the people respond?**

God allowed the Amorites to defeat and chase them. The people cried — but God paid no attention. The image is almost of whining children who expect a response simply by making enough noise. Obedience is not negotiable after the fact.

**Q10. How would you summarise the causes of the Israelites' rebellion in ch1?**

Fear, doubting God's goodness and faithfulness, and presumption. Draw out the connection: all three are failures of trust — treating God as less than he has shown himself to be.

---

### 2:1–23 — The Faithfulness of God in the Wilderness

**Q11. Summarise 2:1–23.**

God is the one who gives and removes kingdoms. Even in the wilderness years, he was at work — directing Israel's path around nations whose land he had given them, providing for his people, and governing the movements of peoples they had no dealings with.

**Q12. How does Moses summarise their time in the wilderness (vv7–8)? What is the purpose of this summary?**

God has blessed them, watched over them, and been with them throughout. They need not fear now that they are about to enter the land — the same God who sustained them for forty years will sustain them for what lies ahead.

**Q13. What was Israel meant to learn from the conquests of the Moabites, Horites, and Ammonites? How had these nations conquered?**

The descendants of Lot and Esau were also given land by the Lord and drove out those who were there before them. The Lord is the one who gives kingdoms and victory. If God did this for these pagan nations, how much more will he do it for his own people? The implication is sharp: there is no reason to doubt.

---

### 2:24–3:11 — The Lord Gives Victory

*(This section is discussed in small groups using the handout — see below.)*

**What is most striking about the defeat of these two kings?**

The completeness and ease of it. Sihon and Og were formidable opponents — Og in particular is described as the last of the Rephaim, a giant whose bed was enormous (3:11). Their defeat was total and, from Israel's perspective, almost effortless. What had seemed terrifying turned out to be no obstacle to God.

**What is the role of the Lord in their defeat? How does Moses emphasise this?**

The Lord gives them into Israel's hands; the Lord is fighting for them. Moses uses language that makes it clear: the victory is God's, not Israel's. They are participants in something God is doing, not achievers in their own right.

**Why was it important for the Israelites to be reminded of these victories? How would this encourage them?**

Because they are about to face similar opponents. The memory of past victories is not nostalgia — it is evidence. God has shown he can do this. He will do it again.

---

### 3:12–29 — Entering the Land

**Q15. What are the Reubenites and Gadites commanded to do? What does this say about the nature of the people of God?**

Not to settle in their lands east of the Jordan until the whole land has been taken for the rest of the people. Solidarity among God's people is required: they are one people, and individual blessing does not permit abandonment of the community. This is worth dwelling on — it is countercultural in any era.

**Q16. What is the mood at the end of chapter 3? Why?**

Hopeful but sombre. There is genuine encouragement — Joshua will lead them, God will be with them. But Moses will not enter the land. The consequences of past sin and failure are still real, even for the greatest leaders. This acts as a warning without extinguishing hope.

---

### Application Questions

*(Discussed in small groups. These questions are designed to move from observation to personal reflection and prayer.)*

**Can you think of ways in which we, as Christians, might be tempted to disobey God because of fear, doubt, or presumption? Be practical.**

Encourage specific, honest examples. Fear might look like: not sharing faith, not making a costly decision God has clearly called someone to, not trusting God with finances. Presumption might look like: assuming God's blessing on choices made without reference to his word, or expecting forgiveness without repentance. Doubt might look like: acting as though prayer doesn't really change things, or hedging obedience with an escape route.

**How does Moses' sermon to the Israelites help us avoid falling into disobedience as Christians?**

The pattern of look-at-the-track-record is directly transferable. We have an even fuller record than Israel — not just exodus and wilderness, but cross and resurrection. If God did not spare his own Son, will he not give us all things? (Romans 8:32)

**What promises of the Lord are we currently struggling to believe? How does reading about his power and faithfulness encourage us?**

This question is personal and may provoke silence. Give it time. The willingness to name unbelief honestly is itself a step toward trust.

**How does Moses point us to Jesus in today's passage? What does that mean for us?**

Moses as mediator and intercessor; Moses bearing consequences partly rooted in others' sin; Moses seeing the land he will not enter — all of this points forward to one who is both greater and more complete. Jesus is the mediator who does enter the Promised Land, who bears the full consequence of others' sin, and who leads his people home. See Hebrews 3:1–6 if helpful.

---

### Small Group Handouts

**Handout A: Deuteronomy 1:6–18**

*What do vv6–8 show us about the character of God?*
*(See notes above — God initiates, directs, and gives.)*

*At first glance vv9–18 might seem to have a mainly organisational focus with little application. What does it say about what God values? How will these values be picked up later in the book?*
*(See notes above — justice, impartiality, fair hearing; recurs in chs 16–17, 19.)*

*Why do you think it was important for the Israelites to be reminded about this leadership structure at this stage in their history?*
*(See notes above — they need good structures for the new phase of life in the land.)*

**Handout B: Deuteronomy 2:24–3:11**

*What is most striking about the defeat of these two kings?*
*(See notes above.)*

*What is the role of the Lord in their defeat? How does Moses emphasise this?*
*(See notes above.)*

*Why do you think it was important for the Israelites to be reminded of these victories? How would this encourage them?*
*(See notes above.)*

**Shared Application (all groups)**

*Can you think of ways in which we, as Christians, might be tempted to disobey God because of fear, doubt, or presumption? Be practical.*

*How does Moses' sermon to the Israelites help us avoid falling into disobedience as Christians?*

*What promises of the Lord are we currently struggling to believe? How does reading about his power and faithfulness encourage us?*

*How does Moses point us to Jesus in today's passage? What does that mean for us?*

paras: Julie Rowbory grew up in Northern Ireland and was educated at the University of Cambridge, where she obtained an MA and then an MPhil in history. She has worked as a Latin and history teacher, taught English in Asia and lived in East Africa. She is married to David, with whom she has four daughters. They divide their time between Scotland and West Africa.

studyAiData.aiMentorPrompt: You are a warm, thoughtful mentor for a Bible study series called ‘1 John for Today’ — exploring the New Testament book of 1 John.
